title: Default Key Combinations
text: Default Key Combinations A complete set of keystroke combinations is available in all the keyboard translation tables supplied in the USYS library. These keystroke combinations are defined by the macro ^GOLD_BASE_SET and are the same for every keyboard, unless they are not defined in the keyboard translation table being used. These, or default mappings of your own, should be included in any keyboard translation table you create. The macro ^GOLD_BASE_SET includes the following definitions: Subfield separator Profile characters Gold key combinations that allow keyboard entry of simple structure editor functions Super key (by default, Gold Space ) Super key combinations that allow keyboard entry composite structure editor functions HT ^FIELD SOH ^REFRESH Copy The availability of these combinations allows relative keyboard independence (once you have located the Gold key) and is especially useful if you regularly use different terminals. In your own keyboard translation tables, you should first map the location of the Gold key, then use the macro ^GOLD_BASE_SET to define the complete set of keystroke combinations in one mapping. If the Gold key has not been defined when you use ^GOLD_BASE_SET , the compilation fails. To include only some of the keystroke combinations defined by ^Gold_BASE_SET , either disable the ones you prefer not to use or define only the ones you need. Example to disable the key combinations for erase in the MSWINX keyboard translation table: Ensure that the MSWINX keyboard translation table ^136+ and ^131^101 are mapped to GOLD key. In the MSWINX keyboard translation table, at the bottom add: Explain this code ^130E ^NUL ^130e ^NUL ^136+E ^NUL ^131^101E ^NUL ^136+e ^NUL ^131^101e ^NUL Copy For more information, see Gold and Super Key Combinations . To redefine the subfield separator or a profile character, you should map the new definition to the appropriate position in Font 0. For more information, see Subfield Separator and Profile Characters . The following example creates the complete set of Gold and SUPER key combinations, using the Escape key as Gold: ^ESC ^GOLD ^CHR ^GOLD_BASE_SET Copy By default, the SUPER key is mapped to Gold SPACE. If you prefer, you can define the SUPER key as a single key, such as a function key. For example, on a VT200-compatible keyboard, to change the definition for the SUPER key to PF9, define the following: ^CHR ^GOLD_BASE_SET ^esc[20~ ^SUPER Copy To use Gold / instead of Gold Space on an NCD keyboard, define: ^CHR ^GOLD_BASE_SET ^ESCOk/ ^SUPER Copy Was this topic helpful?
